titleTag: Michigan Landlord Tenant Law 2026 | Rights, Deposits & Eviction
metaDescription: Michigan landlord tenant law 2026: security deposit limits, eviction notice requirements, tenant rights & more. Plain-language guide for new landlords.

sections.heading: Security deposit rules
body: If you're looking for Michigan security deposit rules, focus on (1) whether there is a cap, (2) when the deposit must be returned, and (3) whether an itemized statement is required.
bullets: Deposit cap: 2 months' rent
Return deadline: 14 days after move-out/tenancy end (deductions can affect details).
Itemized statement: required if deductions are taken (check deadlines and delivery method).
Deductions: Varies — confirm current statute.
Penalty risk: Varies — confirm current statute.
Interest: Varies — confirm current statute.

sections.heading: Eviction notice requirements
body: For Michigan eviction notice requirements, the notice period often depends on why the eviction is being pursued (nonpayment vs lease violation vs holdover). Always use the correct notice form and follow the court process.
bullets: Nonpayment notice: varies — confirm current statute and required notice form.
Nonpayment notice type: Varies — confirm required notice form (pay-or-quit, demand for possession, etc.).
Lease violation notice: varies — confirm cure rights and timelines.
Lease violation notice type: Varies — confirm notice type and cure rules.
Cure allowed: varies
Month-to-month termination: commonly 7 days (verify tenancy type).
Holdover/termination notice type: Varies — confirm termination/holdover notice rules.
Court process: confirm whether judicial eviction is required (most states prohibit self-help).
Self-help eviction: varies — confirm current statute and case law.
Timeline:

Court steps: {"statutes":"MCL 554.134; MCL 600.5750-600.5805 (Summary Proceeding for Eviction)","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"7-day notice to pay or vacate for residential tenants (MCL 600.5750)","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy (MCL 554.134)","lease_violation":"7-day notice for noncompliance with material lease terms; 30-day notice for repeated violations","no_cause":"No statewide just cause requirement; Detroit local ordinance requires just cause for covered units"},"full_text":"MCL 554.134. Termination of tenancy. A tenancy from year to year shall be terminated by a notice of 1 year to quit, given not less than 3 months before the end of the year. A tenancy from month to month shall be terminated by a notice of 1 month to quit, given not less than 1 month before the date on which the tenancy is to terminate.\nMCL 600.5750. Summary proceeding; residential premises. (1) A landlord may bring a summary proceeding to recover possession of premises if the tenant fails to pay rent when due and has not paid within 7 days after the landlord gives written notice requiring the payment of rent or possession of the premises. (2) The summons shall be made returnable not less than 7 nor more than 10 days after service.\nMCL 600.5752. Judgment for landlord. If the court finds that rent is due and unpaid, or that the tenant has violated a material term of the lease, the court may issue a judgment for possession and restitution of the premises."}

sections.heading: Landlord entry notice
body: If you're searching for Michigan landlord entry notice rules, look for (1) required notice time, (2) allowed entry reasons, and (3) emergency exceptions.
bullets: Notice: often governed by lease terms and a reasonableness standard (state law may not set a single hour-count).
Typical notice time: varies; many leases use 24 hours as a practical baseline.
Allowed reasons: Varies — commonly inspections, repairs, services, and showing the unit.
Timing: Varies — usually at reasonable times.
Emergencies: generally allow entry without advance notice.
Tenant refusal: varies
Remedies/penalties: Varies — confirm remedies (injunction, damages, attorney fees) under state law.

sections.heading: Rent increase rules
body: For Michigan rent increase rules, most states focus on notice requirements, while rent caps are usually local (or limited to specific covered housing).
bullets: Statewide rent control: typically none, but local/covered-housing rules may apply.
Local rent control: may exist in certain cities/counties (check if your property is covered).
Notice of rent increase: varies — confirm current statute and any local ordinances.
Fixed-term leases: Varies — confirm your lease terms and statute.
Month-to-month: Varies — generally allowed with proper notice.
Delivery: Varies — confirm written notice and delivery method requirements.
Just cause: varies
Retaliation: Retaliation protections may limit rent increases after protected tenant complaints; confirm local/state rules.

sections.heading: Tenant rights & habitability basics
body: Most Michigan landlord tenant law disputes involve habitability, repairs, and retaliation protections. Keep repair requests documented and respond promptly.
bullets: Habitability: confirm whether an implied warranty applies
Landlord repair duty: Varies — confirm landlord repair/habitability duties in statute and local code.
Tenant notice: Varies — confirm required notice/cure steps before remedies.
Repair-and-deduct: varies
Rent withholding: varies
Termination: Varies — some states allow termination after notice and failure to repair.
Retaliation: varies
Code enforcement: Varies — contact local housing/code enforcement (city/county) for inspections and official repair orders.

jurisdictionNotes: Local ordinances (city/county) can be stricter than state law.
Rules can change; verify before acting on any single requirement.
